text: The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to divide the gastrocolic ligament and control small gastric vessels. The dissection of the main trunk of the splenic artery is performed with robotic assistance.

The surgeon clamps the main trunk of the splenic artery to reduce the spleen's volume, facilitating safer dissection. The first and second small arterial branches are reconnected to preserve vascularization of the lower spleen, which remains intact. The main trunk of the artery is controlled with a clip, and the clamp is removed to verify the correct dissection of the arterial branches.

The main trunk of the splenic vein is visible, detached from the tail of the pancreas and encircled by a vessel loop for traction. The surgeon gently detaches the tail of the pancreas from the vessels, grasping the main trunk of the artery and vein. The ascending artery and vein are divided, revealing the lower pole of the spleen recovering its vascularization. The vascular hilum of the upper pole of the spleen is detached from the tail of the pancreas.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to perform parenchymal transection along the demarcation line, minimizing bleeding. The lower pole of the spleen is separated from the upper pole, which contains a large cystic lesion. Bipolar coagulation controls small bleeding from the stump, and both inferior and superior ligaments of the spleen are divided. The vessel loops are removed, and the upper part of the spleen is extracted through a small incision using an endobag.

The surgeon reconstructs the splenophrenic ligament to prevent torsion of the remnant spleen on its vascular pole. A hemostatic matrix is applied to control small bleeding from the remaining spleen tissue, completing the procedure.
